planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–October. Jabotilla tolerates light frosts to about 28°F when established, making it marginally cold-hardy for zone 9. In zone 9b–10 it grows as an evergreen specimen and produces fruit seasonally; in zone 9a protect from hard freezes. The tree grows slowly and produces small annual crops of berries on the trunk and branches in the characteristic Myrciaria style.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, acidic, organically rich soil; Jabotilla (also called Guavaberry or Rumberry in the Caribbean) is a medium to large Myrciaria species producing small, round, dark-purple to black berries directly on the trunk and main branches in the same cauliflorous pattern as Jaboticaba — but the fruit is smaller, more tart, and has a distinctive spiced, aromatic quality with notes of cloves, vanilla, and exotic spice that make it highly prized in Caribbean culinary traditions. In the US Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico, jabotilla berries are the foundation of 'guavaberry rum' — a traditional Christmas liqueur in which the berries macerate in rum to produce a spiced, aromatic, deeply colored drink of cultural significance. The fresh berries are also eaten raw, made into jams and jellies, and used as a flavoring for drinks and sauces. The tree is ornamental year-round with attractive bark, shiny leaves, and the unusual cauliflorous fruiting pattern. In zone 9 it is a specimen fruit tree for collectors and enthusiasts.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Myrciaria Species for Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Caribbean Fruit Trees for South Florida
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Myrciaria floribunda

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Longue d'Aout (Long of August) is named for its August ripening peak in France. In zone 9 it ripens July–September. The elongated, pear-shaped fruits distinguish it visually from round-fruited varieties. It produces moderate breba crops and abundant main crops.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Longue d'Aout is an old French heirloom fig variety named for its August ripening season, producing distinctive elongated, pear-to-tear-drop-shaped fruits with violet to purple-brown skin and amber to pink-tinged flesh with a sweet, mild, honey-fig flavor and very few seeds. The unusual elongated shape (2–3 inches long, narrowing to a pronounced neck) makes this variety immediately identifiable among more common round-fruited fig varieties and creates an elegant presentation at table. Longue d'Aout is a main-crop variety producing its primary harvest in August–September in zone 9. It grows as a moderately vigorous tree with good productivity once established. This variety is well-regarded in French fig traditions and has been grown for centuries in the south of France; its elongated form makes it particularly distinctive for display and specialty market use.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — French Heirloom Fig Varieties
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fig Cultivar Descriptions
Louisiana State University AgCenter — European Fig Varieties for Louisiana

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. White Genoa is productive in zone 9 and grows well in the Gulf Coast climate. In zone 9 it produces a main crop in August–September and may have a light breba crop in June. The light-colored fruit skin does not camouflage ripeness well — monitor for softening as the harvest cue.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; White Genoa is an Italian light-skinned fig producing medium to large, greenish-yellow to pale gold fruits with amber to pink-tinged flesh and a mild, sweet, delicate flavor — one of the milder-flavored fig varieties, with a honey-sweet taste that lacks the robust winey complexity of dark-skinned varieties but is exceptionally appealing for fresh eating by people who find strong fig flavor too intense. The pale skin and light flesh of White Genoa produce an elegant presentation as fresh fruit or in light-colored preserves; it makes an outstanding pale-gold fig jam. It is widely grown in Australia and California where it performs well in Mediterranean-type climates and is considered one of the best light-skinned figs for fresh eating. In zone 9 and the Gulf Coast, White Genoa performs comparably to its reputation in warm Mediterranean climates. The light-colored fruits are prone to souring and cracking if they crack near the eye in wet weather; plant in locations with good air circulation.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — White Genoa Fig
University of California Cooperative Extension — Light-Skinned Fig Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Figs for Louisiana

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Noire de Caromb is a main-crop fig ripening August–September in zone 9. It is a moderately vigorous tree well-suited to zone 9 heat. The dark fruit absorbs heat well and ripens thoroughly even in partial shade conditions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Noire de Caromb is a French dark-skinned fig from the Caromb commune in Vaucluse, Provence — part of the rich fig heritage of Provence where the warm, dry Mediterranean climate has sustained diverse fig cultivation for millennia. The medium-sized, round to slightly flattened fruits have very dark purple to nearly black skin and deep red-to-garnet flesh with an intensely rich, complex, sweet flavor with pronounced winey depth that characterizes the best Provençal dark figs. Noire de Caromb is considered one of the finest-flavored dark-skinned fig varieties by European fig enthusiasts, though it remains less well-known in North American markets than brown Turkey or Celeste. In zone 9 the Gulf Coast heat produces fig flavor of excellent quality, and Noire de Caromb performs well in the long, warm growing season. Its primary advantage over more common dark-skinned figs is the depth and complexity of flavor.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Provençal Fig Cultivars
University of California Cooperative Extension — Dark-Skinned Fig Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Specialty Fig Varieties for Louisiana

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Violette de Sollies is primarily a fall-ripening fig; in zone 9 the main crop ripens September–October, later than most other varieties. The late ripening extends the fresh fig season into fall. It produces minimal breba fruit and is primarily valued for its exceptional main crop quality.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Violette de Sollies has Protected Designation of Origin (AOP) status as the primary fig of Sollies-Pont commune in Var, Provence — the most commercially important fig production area in France. It accounts for approximately 80% of French commercial fig production and is the fig you encounter in French supermarkets and on French cheese boards. The large, round to pear-shaped fruits have dark violet to nearly black skin and deep red to garnet flesh with an exceptionally creamy, melt-in-the-mouth texture and rich, complex sweetness. The PDO designation reflects the specific microclimate of the Var region and the care taken to produce the variety at optimal quality. Violette de Sollies is a fall-ripening variety, producing its main crop in September–October in zone 9 — later than most other figs and extending the fresh fig season into autumn. The exceptional flavor quality and French culinary prestige make it a premium choice for zone 9 gardens where the warm fall provides ideal ripening conditions.
sources: INAO — Figue de Sollies AOP Documentation
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — French PDO Fig Varieties
University of California Cooperative Extension — Premium Fig Cultivars
